coredata.dc:title: N-doped carbon dot as fluorescent probe for detection of cysteamine and multicolor cell imaging
coredata.dc:creator: Konar, Suraj
Kumar, B.N. Prashanth
Mahto, Madhusudan Kr.
Samanta, Dipanjan
Shaik, Md Abdus Salam
Shaw, Manisha
Mandal, Mahitosh
Pathak, Amita
coredata.dc:description: In the present work, we developed nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) as novel fluorescence probe for the quantitative detection of cysteamine. NCDs were synthesized by a rapid one-pot microwave assisted pyrolysis method using tartaric acid as the source of carbon and urea as N-dopant. The synthesized NCDs possessed multicolor (blue, green and red) fluorescence and excitation wavelength dependent emission behaviour. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of NCDs was quenched with the addition of cysteamine which may be attributed to the formation of non-fluorescent ground state complex. Under optimum condition, the linear response was observed in the range of 10–210 μM and the detection limit was found to be 75.6 nM for sensing the cysteamine. Moreover, the proposed sensing methodology showed high selectivity for cysteamine over a number of interfering metal ions and amino acids. The remarkable biocompatibility of NCDs was investigated in MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells which impacts its immense potential for diagnostic purposes. The proposed sensing method is thus simple with high sensitivity and excellent selectivity. We expect that this method will become a potential tool for the detection of cysteamine in biological samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen doped carbon dots
Cysteamine
multicolor
Fluorescence probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of cysteamine: LOD measurement and explanation of quenching
Optimization of the variables in quenching study
Effect of interfering metal ions and amino acids
Cellular uptake and cytotoxicity studies of NCDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) XRD pattern of NCDs, (B) TEM image (scale bar represents 20 nm) and particle size distribution of NCDs (inset). (C) Raman spectrum of NCDs. (D) FTIR spectrum of NCDs. (E) XPS survey spectrum and (F) XPS C 1 s spectrum of synthesized NCDs.
(A) DLS graph of NCDs. (B) Variation of zeta potential values of NCDs at various pH (2–9). (C) UV–vis absorption spectrum of NCDs dispersed in water.

(A) The quenching of fluorescence intensity (λex = 360 nm) of NCDs with successive addition of cysteamine of various concentrations (0–300 μM). (B) The Stern-Volmer plot for NCDs-cysteamine system. (C) Dependence of F/F0 on the concentrations of cysteamine within the linear range of 0–210 μM.

The influence of (A) various metal ions and (B) amino acids on the fluorescence intensity of CD. F0 stands for the fluorescence intensity of NCDs only (blank) and F represents the fluorescence intensity of amino acids or metal ions or cysteamine (λex = 360 nm, [CD] = 1 mg/mL).

Fluorescence microscopic images for cellular uptake of MDA-MB-231 Cells treated with NCDs at varying time intervals. White arrows indicate the accumulation of the NCDs within cells. Scale bars: 20 μm.
Cytotoxicity studies of NCDs on MDA-MB-231 cells. The experiment was performed in triplicate.
The quenching constants (KSV) for the interaction of the NCDs with cysteamine. Condition: [NCDs] = 30 μg mL−1, [Cysteamine] = 10–210 μM.
Comparison of different analytical methods for the detection of cysteamine.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Abbreviation: HPLC (High performance liquid chromatography), UV (Ultraviolet), CV (Cyclic voltammetry), DPV (differential pulse voltammetry).

head.ce:title: N-doped carbon dot as fluorescent probe for detection of cysteamine and multicolor cell imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur, West Bengal, 721302, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, R.D. & D.J. College Munger, Munger University, Bihar, 811201, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Medical Science and Technology, Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur, West Bengal, 721302, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Medical Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Prepared nitrogen doped carbon dots, a fluorescence based biosensor serve as a rapid, sensitive and selective analytical method to detect clinically important cysteamine in biological samples and the remarkable biocompatibility of NCDs which was investigated in MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells has proven its immense potential toward diagnostic purposes.

In the present work, we developed nitrogen-doped carbon dots (NCDs) as novel fluorescence probe for the quantitative detection of cysteamine. NCDs were synthesized by a rapid one-pot microwave assisted pyrolysis method using tartaric acid as the source of carbon and urea as N-dopant. The synthesized NCDs possessed multicolor (blue, green and red) fluorescence and excitation wavelength dependent emission behaviour. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of NCDs was quenched with the addition of cysteamine which may be attributed to the formation of non-fluorescent ground state complex. Under optimum condition, the linear response was observed in the range of 10–210 μM and the detection limit was found to be 75.6 nM for sensing the cysteamine. Moreover, the proposed sensing methodology showed high selectivity for cysteamine over a number of interfering metal ions and amino acids. The remarkable biocompatibility of NCDs was investigated in MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells which impacts its immense potential for diagnostic purposes. The proposed sensing method is thus simple with high sensitivity and excellent selectivity. We expect that this method will become a potential tool for the detection of cysteamine in biological samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Cysteamine, an aminothiol compound, is perhaps the only therapeutic molecule known for the treatment of cystinosis [1], which is a genetic disease characterized by abnormal accumulation of the amino acid, cysteine, within the lysosomes and then eventually gets crystallized/deposited in the vital organs such as – kidney, liver, spleen, cornea etc. Cysteamine is known to suppress the deposition of cysteine in lysosome by penetrating through the lysosomal membrane and rupturing the SS bonds between cysteine molecules in the deposit and in the process producing free cysteine and cysteine–cysteamine disulfide that gets eliminated through lysine porters [2]. Cysteamine is also recognized for its neuroprotective ability and can function to preserve the neuronal structure and/or function in animal cells. Besides, the thiol functionality in cysteamine assists its oxidizing ability and enhances its potency as an antioxidant [3].

Considering the importance of cysteamine in clinical science, it is important that its concentration is selectively and precisely determined in biological samples. In the past, cysteamine determination has been reported through high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [4–6], ion exchange column chromatography [7], gas chromatography with flame photometry [8], electrophoresis [9], cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) [10–12]. Most often, to increase the sensitivity and selectivity of the detection, these techniques have been used in combination; this however makes the procedure more complex and elaborate besides being time-inefficient and laborious. The conventional spectrophotometric based detection techniques, which are credited to be rapid, selective and sensitive, lose their relevance for the detection of cysteamine (or its derivatives) due to lack of chromophoric groups in the compound. However, in recent years, detection of cysteamine has been made possible through spectrophotometric methods by the inclusion of suitable optical probe along with the analyte in the samples. For example, Apyari et al. reported the detection of cysteamine through UV–vis spectroscopy [13] using chromogenic gold nanoparticles tagged cysteamine where the gold nanoparticles served as an optical probe.

Of recent, carbon quantum dots (CDs) have gained significant importance as optical sensors/ probes owing to their selective, excitation wavelength (λex) dependent photoluminescence (PL) properties [14]. Their small size (< 10 nm) with uniform distribution and the polyaromatic carbogenic core with numerous surface (emissive) trap sites contributes to their unique photoluminescence properties [15]. Thus, the CDs can be exploited as a fluorescent probe for the detection of analytes even in the absence of chromophoric groups in the analyte samples. The sensing mechanism of these probes is typically based on monitoring the change in the fluorescence intensity of the CDs induced by the analytes through charge transfer that caused non-radiative electron/hole recombination or annihilation through surface quenched photo-induced state [16]. Moreover, their good water solubility, stability in almost all common solvents and biocompatibility makes them favorable for applications as optical sensors/ probes for detection of analytes in biological samples as well as for their in vitro and in vivo optical imaging. Selective and sensitive detection of various metal ions [17,18], anions [19,20], hydroquinone [21], picric acid [22], sugar [16], biological resources such as melamine [23], glucose [24], ascorbic acid [25], nucleic acid [26], protein [27], thrombin [28], cholesterol [29], glutathione [30,31] etc. have been already reported using CDs as a fluorescence probe. However, to date there are no reports on fluorescence-based detection of cysteamine using carbon dot as the optical probe.

The only hindrance in the widespread exploitation of CDs as an optical probe is due to their lower luminescence intensity (i.e., the quantum yield) compared to the established, quantum dots containing cadmium or other heavy metals or the molecular probes. Current literature on CDs asserts that their luminescence intensity can be considerably enhanced through passivation of the surface through conjugation with suitable electron donor groups or by doping them with various electron rich heteroatoms such as N [15], B [16], S [32] and P [33]. Of these atoms, the most pronounced augmentation in fluorescence intensity of CDs has been reported on doping with highly electronegative nitrogen atom possibly due to synergistic charge transfer between the electron-rich nitrogen atom and electron deficient carbon species [34]. Additionally, the functional groups incorporated onto the CDs surfaces from the decomposition of nitrogen source compound tend to passivate their surface and hence contribute towards the enhancement in their fluorescence intensity.

Till date, luminescent CDs have been synthesized through various techniques such as-arc-discharge, laser-ablation, electrochemical oxidation, combustion, thermal decomposition and microwave-assisted pyrolysis. Among these, the microwave-assisted pyrolysis method emerges as one of the most appropriate methods for the synthesis of CDs [35]. The method is not only convenient and technically simple but can also be effortlessly applied to scale up the yield and to introduce various dopant atoms into CDs for tuning their fluorescence properties.

The present study, therefore, focuses on the synthesis of photoluminescent N-doped CDs through the rapid, simple and cost-effective microwave pyrolysis method using tartaric acid and urea as the starting materials. Further, the synthesized multicolour fluorescent (blue, green and red), water-soluble NCDs have been explored as a fluorescent probe for rapid, selective and sensitive detection of cysteamine by monitoring the quenching the fluorescence intensity. Also, the synthesized NCDs were successfully employed in multicolor imaging of MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells.

In a typical synthetic procedure, at first, 1 g tartaric acid (Merck Ltd, Mumbai, 99.7%) and 1 g urea (S.D. fine chemical Ltd, India, 99%) were added to 10 mL milli-Q water, stirred to get a transparent solution. The solution was then heated in a microwave oven at 800 W for 3 min, during which it changed from colorless liquid to brown and finally dark-brown foamy solid, indicating the formation of CDs. The carbogenic solid was dissolved in water and dialyzed for 24 h; the CDs were collected from the supernatant liquid and concentrated through evaporation of the solvent. To initiate the dehydration and carbonization process, tartaric acid was used as a carbon source (containing carboxylic acid group) and urea as a surface passivating agent [36].

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of synthesized NCDs was recorded using a Bruker AXS Diffractometer D8 powder XRD equipped with Cu-Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å) with an applied voltage of 40 kV and a scan rate of 3°/min. The morphology and particle size of synthesized NCDs were visualized by transmission electron microscope (TEM) using TECNAI G2 20S-TWIN (Japan) with an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. The size distribution of NCDs was calculated from TEM image by using ImageJ software (version 1.33; National Institutes of Health, USA). The Raman spectrum of NCDs was carried out by a HORIBA JobinYvon T64000 Raman spectrometer by using a laser source (Ar-Kr) of fixed wavelength 514 nm. To ensure the presence of passivating agents in NCDs surface, Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectrum was recorded as KBr pellets by Perkin-Elmer Spectrum RX-II (Model no. 73713, USA) within the scan range of 4000–400 cm−1. X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) was obtained on a PHI-5000 Versa probe II spectrometer with an Al Kα X-Ray monochromator (1486.7 eV). The binding energy scale was calibrated by the standard value of C1 s at 284.6 eV. The hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential of the sample at different pH were determined by using Malvern Nano ZS instrument (Germany). The tests were repeated thrice in order to gain precise results. The absorption spectrum of NCDs was obtained by using UV-vis spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU UV-2450) and PL emission studies were recorded on Fluorescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI F-7000). The normalized fluorescence spectrum at different excitation wavelengths was obtained by dividing each fluorescence intensity point-by-point by the maximum intensity value. The time-resolved fluorescence lifetime was measured with a time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) device. A picoseconds diode laser (IBH, UK, Nanoled) was used as light source at the excitation wavelength of 375 nm. The signal was detected in magic angle (54.7°) polarization using a Hamamastsu MCP PMT (3809U) and the decays were analyzed using IBH DAS-6 decay analysis software.

To determine the quantum yield of the NCDs, quinine sulphate (Sigma-Aldrich, Germany, 99%) was chosen as a standard in 0.1 M H2SO4 (literature quantum yield 54%) [36]. The optical densities were measured on UV–vis spectrophotometer. The quantum yield of NCDs in water was calculated according to the equation:
∅
∅
S t d
=
A
S t d
A
×
I
I
S t d
×
n 2
n
S t d
2
Where ∅ is the quantum yield, I is the measured integrated emission intensity, n is the refractive index (1.33 for water), and A is the optical density (O.D.). The subscript “Std” refers to the reference fluorophore (here, quinine sulphate) of known quantum yield. The O.D. was kept less than 0.1 to avoid inner filter effects.

For more accurate calculations, the following equation can be used, where “Slope” represents the slope of the plot of optical density versus integrated intensity [36].
∅ =
∅
S t d
×
S l o p e
S l o p e
S t d
×
n 2
n
S t d
2

A stock solution of cysteamine hydrochloride (Sigma-Aldrich, 99%) of 1 mL, 1 mM was prepared in phosphate buffer (1 mM, pH 7). For the detection of cysteamine, the stock solution of NCDs (30 μg/mL) was also prepared in phosphate buffer (1 mM, pH 7). Various amount of cysteamine (10–300 μM) was added into different sets of NCDs stock solution. The resultant mixture was incubated for 5 min at room temperature and then the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded from 380 nm to 700 nm at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm with the slit widths of 5/5 nm. Each set of the solution was prepared and the emission spectrum was recorded repeatedly for three times.

The stock solutions of various metal ions (1 mL, 1 mM) such as, K+, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Cu2+ and Fe3+; various amino acids such as, glycine, alanine, leucine, tryptophan, glutamine, serine, cysteine, glutamic acid and histidine were dispersed in phosphate buffer (1 mM, pH 7). For examining the selective cysteamine detection study, a fixed concentration of metal ions/amino acids stock solution were mixed into the NCDs solution (30 μg/mL) in phosphate buffer (1 mM, pH 7); incubated for 5 min at room temperature and then the fluorescence spectra were recorded under excitation at 360 nm. The effect of fluorescence intensity of NCDs solution by the addition of Na+ ion was carried out in milli-Q water.

MDA-MB-231 cells (1.5 × 104) were seeded on each sterile cover slips placed in 60 mm petri plate and allowed to adhere for attaining the spread morphology. The medium was treated with 30 μL NCDs (1 mg/mL) in the culture medium. Further, the cells were allowed to incubate at varying time intervals of 15, 45 and 90 min. The cells were washed twice with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, Gibco Invitrogen, India) and fixed with 3.7% para-formaldehyde. Subsequent dehydration steps were performed in 50, 70, 95 and 100% ethanol for 5 min each followed by permanent mounting with DPX for confocal microscopic imaging (CLSM, Olympus FluoView FV1000) [37,38]. The sample was then focused and observed on a confocal microscope with imaging software, FV10-ASW2.0 Viewer.

Cell proliferation assay was used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of NCDs in MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cells. The cells (5 × 103 per well) were seeded in 96-well flat-bottom plates for 24 h at 37 °C and then treated with NCDs at varying concentrations (0–250 μg/mL) and then incubated for 48 h. After incubation, the medium containing suspended carriers was removed and replaced by 80 μL of 1 mg/mL 3-(4, 5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl) - 2, 5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT, Sigma-Aldrich, 98%) solution and allowed to incubate at 37 °C for 4 h. Further, the formed formazan crystals were dissolved in 100 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, Sigma-Aldrich) in each well. The absorbance of all samples was measured at 570 nm using a microplate reader and the cell viability was calculated by standard procedure [39,40]. The assay was performed three times to get the precise result.

The XRD pattern of synthesized NCDs displays a sharp, highly intense diffraction peak centered at 27.2°(2θ value) corresponding to the (002) lattice planeas shown in Fig.1 A. The interlayer spacing distance of (002) plane is found to be around 3.28 Å, which is analogous to the (002) lattice spacing (3.34 Å) of graphite, indicating the formation of crystallized carbon [41]. The crystallized carbon is formed by the aggregation of the carbon nuclei which were grown at high temperature due to continuous carbonization process.

TEM has been used as an influential tool to determine the morphology of nanoscale materials. The TEM image of NCDs shows that CD particles are fairly spherical in shape and exhibits a homogeneous distribution in a polymeric matrix. The size distribution of the particles is estimated by using ImageJ software and graphically represented in Fig. 1B (inset). The mean particle diameter of NCDs is measured to be 3 nm. Owing to their ultrasmall size, synthesized NCDs are dispersed in water without ultra-sonication with transparent appearance and acknowledged to be ‘water-soluble CD’.

The Raman spectrum of synthesized NCDs is depicted in Fig. 1C. The spectrum shows two broad peaks at around 1598 cm−1 corresponding to the G band (sp2 hybridized) and 1350 cm−1 due to the D band (sp3 hybridized). The G band is associated with the vibration of sp2-hybridized carbon atoms in the 2-D hexagonal lattice of graphite and D band corresponds to the vibration of sp3-hybridized carbon atoms in the termination plane of disordered graphite [42]. The relative intensity of disordered D band and graphitic G band (ID/IG) is found to be 0.81 indicating the formation of disordered graphitic nature of NCDs.

The FTIR spectrum of NCDs was recorded to analyze the surface composition and the spectrum is shown in Fig.1D. In the spectrum a broad absorption band centered at around 3434 cm−1 is seen, which can be associated with OH stretching vibration of carboxylic moiety [43] and strong absorption band at 3185 cm−1 can be attributed to the vibrational stretch of NH bond indicating the nitrogen doping to the NCDs [44]. The absorption peaks at around 2925 and 2870 cm−1 are related to the CH stretching mode of vibrations [45]. Whereas the absorption peak at around 1119 and a shoulder at 1720 cm−1 can be assigned to COH stretching and CO or CN vibrational stretch respectively [46–48]. In addition, the peaks appeared at 1398 and 1075 cm−1 assigned to bending mode of CO and stretching mode of C–O–C respectively [45]. The characteristic absorption band of the −CONR vibration mode at around 1665 cm−1 is observed due to the formation of the amide group during the passivation process [49]. The above findings provide evidence about the existence of −OH, –C = O, −COOH, –C = N, –NH2 and −CONR groups on the surface of NCDs and thus it imparts excellent water-solubility and excellent PL property [50].

XPS measurement was carried out to investigate the surface elemental features of NCDs. The full-range XPS spectrum of NCDs shows C1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s peaks corresponding to the binding energy values of 284.4 eV, 398.8 eV and 530.5 eV respectively (Fig. 1E). The XPS result confirms the presence of nitrogen in the sample.In the extended spectra of C1 s (shown in Fig. 1F) the peaks at 284.4, 285.7, 287.8 and 288.2 eV can be assigned to CC (sp3)/CC (sp2), C–N (sp3), CO (sp2) and OC = C (sp2)/CN (sp2), respectively [36,51]. The N1 s XPS signal at 398.8 eV indicates the formation of pyrrolic N, where the intramolecular dehydration between the neighbor carboxyl and amide group is responsible for the formation of pyrrolic N structure [52]. The O1s XPS signal at 530.5 eV can be attributed to the presence of CO, COC, COH groups, indicating the presence of carbonyl and carboxylic groups on NCDs surface. The above result was well consistent with the results of FTIR.

The DLS measurement shows that the hydrodynamic diameter of NCDs is found to be 300.7 ± 5 nm as manifested in Fig.2 A. The unanticipated escalating value of the hydrodynamic diameter of NCDs is due to their formation in the polymeric matrix (confirmed by morphological study). The excellent aqueous solubility of NCDs for six months without precipitation is confirmed by the zeta potential measurement. It is presumably due to the electrostatic repulsion between the molecules, for the high negative value of zeta potential (–22.5 mV) in an aqueous medium (pH = 7), the assembly of NCDs to form aggregated carbon nanoparticles is hindered. The isoelectric point (IEP) of synthesized CD is found to be 2.9 (Fig.2B). The lower value of IEP suggesting the surface of NCDs is surrounded by plenty of negatively charged groups.

Typical UV–vis absorption curve of NCDs is displayed in Fig. 2C. The spectrum shows two broad shoulders centered at around 355 nm and 285 nm and expands to 600 nm. According to the previous reports, the broad band at 355 nm may be ascribed to the n → π* transition of functional groups with CO [53], where as another broad band at around 285 nm is attributed to the π → π* transition of CC bond and CN bonds. The carboxyl and carbonyl functional groups may be originated from the surface of NCDs, while the CC from the core situated in sp2 hybridized polymeric domains [54,55].

The detailed analysis of the emission properties of NCDs is illustrated in Fig. S1. The excitation wavelength (λex) and concentration dependent emission behaviour of aqueous NCDs solution is shown in Fig.S1 A to S1D. At low concentration (30 μg/mL) of NCDs, when the λex is increased from 320 nm to 480 nm (20 nm step length), the corresponding emission maxima (λem) is shifted from 460 nm (indigo) to 540 nm (green). Whereas, at high concentration (10 mg/mL) with increasing λex value from 380 nm to 560 nm the corresponding λem is shifted from 520 nm (green) to 640 nm (red). The multicolored luminescent nature of NCDs may arise due to three reasons: (1) development of nanoparticles aggregates in a polymeric matrix, (2) radiative recombination of surface-confined electrons and holes by efficient photo induced charge separations in the particle surface [56] and (3) urea, the surface passivating agent which can stabilize the surface sites and generates effective radiative recombination process [57,58]. The brightening phenomenon of photoluminescent NCDs is reflected from the elevated emission quantum yield value, i.e., 7% using quinine sulphate in 0.1 M H2SO4 as a reference at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm.

To achieve excellent fluorescence property in NCDs, the addition of urea is desirable as a passivating agent. Thus the concentration effect of urea has been studied in series of samples where tartaric acid and urea concentration were kept in 3:1, 2:1, 1:1 and 1:2 (w/w) ratios. It is observed that the concentration of urea is proportional to the fluorescence intensity upto a fixed concentration (shown in Fig. S1E). The 1:2 (w/w) ratio is sufficient to achieve the maximum fluorescence intensity. At higher concentrations of urea (i.e., 1:3) no significant enhancement of fluorescence intensity is observed, while the emission peak is red shifted slightly may be due to the formation of particle aggregates (data not shown). Therefore, 1:1 (w/w) ratio of urea and tartaric acidis used throughout the experiments to minimize the aggregation and to achieve the bright fluorescence.

Furthermore, we have analyzed the strongest emission wavelength corresponding to the different values of λex for a series of NCDs solutions in various concentrations. As the concentration of NCDs is changed from 30 μL 0.1 mg/mL to 30 μL 10 mg/mL the λem values are shifted to longer wavelengths as well as lowering the fluorescence intensity (shown in Fig.S1 F). According to Zhang et al., this characteristic feature of CDs is due to their photon reabsorption phenomenon which leads to the aggregation of CDs [59]. Firstly, small sized CDs absorb the incoming photon energy and after some time the excited CDs emit photon having smaller energy than incident one due to the phenomenon known as ‘Stokes shift’. Larger CDs in the solution again absorbs that photon and emitted another photon with a smaller energy than previous one after a similar delay time. Thus, the photon reabsorption technique is developed strongly for dense solution; as a result, the λem red shifted with increasing the concentration of CDs. On the other hand, owing to the lower energy photon emission of larger CD (Stokes shift) along with nonradiative relaxation of the particles the fluorescence intensity is decreased with increasing concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of cysteamine: LOD measurement and explanation of quenching
ce:section.ce:para: The intense fluorescence nature of NCDs was utilized for quantitative detection of cysteamine in aqueous solution. The maximum emission peak of NCDs was gradually quenched with the successive addition of cysteamine at room temperature in the phosphate buffer solution (pH = 7.0) as shown in Fig.3 A. No spectral shift of the maximum emission peak of NCDs was observed during the quenching process.

The fluorescence quenching of NCDs by cysteamine can be classified as either static quenching or dynamic quenching mechanism. The static quenching caused by the close contact between fluorophore (NCDs) and the quencher (cysteamine) before excitation with the formation of non-fluorescent ground-state complex; and dynamic quenching caused by collisional encounters between excited fluorophore and quencher. These two types of quenching mechanism can be distinguished by their dependence on excited-state lifetime and temperature [60]. As the stability of the complex is decreased with increase in temperature, the value of static quenching constant is expected to decrease. However, dynamic quenching constant is going to be increased with increasing temperature because higher temperature results in larger diffusion coefficient and facilitate the process of collisions between fluorophore and quencher. To understand the mechanism of fluorescence quenching, we have performed the quenching experiments at three different temperatures 283, 298 and 323 K and calculated the value of fluorescence quenching constant (KSV) using Stern–Volmer equation
F0/F = 1 + KSV[Q] = 1+ Kqτ0 [Q]
where F0 is the fluorescence intensity of CD in the absence of cysteamine, F is the fluorescence intensity after quenching with the addition of cysteamine, KSV is the Stern–Volmer constant, Q is the cysteamine concentration, Kq is the quenching rate constant and τ0 is the fluorescence lifetime of the fluorophore (NCDs).

The value of F0/F increased gradually with increasing the concentration of cysteamine and the calibration curve at three temperatures shows a linear relationship (In Fig. 3B only 298 K calibration curve is shown) over the inspected concentration range of 10–210 μM of cysteamine. At 298 K, the slope of the curve is found to be 3.54 × 104M−1, which represents the value of KSV, the correlation coefficient is found to be 0.98 and the limit of detection (LOD) is calculated by using the 3σ criterion [61] (where ‘σ’ is the standard deviation and the graph represented in Fig. 3C) which is found to be 75.6 nM. Moreover, to obtain a precise result, we have performed the quenching study in a series of 5 samples and calculated the relative standard deviation (RSD), which is found to be 2.9%.

The Stern–Volmer constant values for the quenching of NCDs fluorescence by cysteamine at different temperatures are shown in Table 1 . The KSV values for the interactions of NCDs with cysteamine are decreased with increasing temperature, indicating that the probable fluorescence quenching process follows a static quenching mechanism rather than a dynamic quenching. Furthermore, time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) is used to study the exciton behavior of the NCDs in the presence and absence of cysteamine. Before adding cysteamine, the lifetime of NCDs (τ0) is found to be 2.9 ns and after the addition of cysteamine, the lifetime of the NCDs is unchanged which supports the static quenching mechanism. In addition, diffusion-controlled quenching constant (Kq) is calculated by considering the fluorescence quenching of NCDs by I− ion and it is found to be about 1.0 × 1010M−1 s−1. As shown in Table 1, at three different temperatures the Kq values are greater than the value of Kq which again indicates that the quenching of NCDs by cysteamine is caused by static quenching mechanism.

The higher values of Kq than the diffusion-controlled limit usually indicate the possibility of various types of binding interaction in between fluorophore and quencher. The high Kq value may result from interaction in between NCDs with cysteamine via electrostatic attraction. The possible mechanistic explanation of unique sensitivity of negatively charged NCDs (IEP 2.9) for cysteamine (IEP 9.5) can be speculated from the view point of simple electrostatic interaction between plenty of deprotonated carboxylic acid groups (NCD−COO−) on the surface of NCDs and protonated amine groups of cysteamine (cysteamine–NH3 +) in phosphate buffer solution at experimental pH 7.0. The electrostatic interaction of NCDs and cysteamine is experimentally proved from the results obtained by zeta potential measurements. The zeta potential of NCD is found to be −22.5 mV at pH 7.0 buffer solution. However, upon addition of 300 μM cysteamine, the zeta potential value of NCD-cysteamine nanocomposite is measured to be −3.8 mV. Therefore, the addition of cysteamine into the NCDs solution induces close contact between fluorophore and quencher via electrostatic interaction; facilitates the formation of NCD-cysteamine ground state complex, leading to quench the fluorescence intensity of NCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the variables in quenching study

ce:section.ce:para: The effect of fluorescence quenching of NCDs in absence and presence of the cysteamine was studied by varying the concentration of the NCDs (from 5.0 to50.0 μg mL−1) at a fixed concentration of cysteamine (100 μM). As shown in Fig. S2A, the difference in fluorescence intensity of NCDs in the absence (F0) and presence of cysteamine (F),i.e., F0-F is increased gradually with the increase in the concentration of NCDs to the maximum concentration of 30.0 μg mL−1 and then F0-F is dropped quickly at higher concentrations of NCDs. The reduction of fluorescence intensity may be attributed to the self-quenching that takes place at high concentration due to the aggregation quenching effect [60]. Therefore, the concentration of NCDs was chosen of 30.0 μg mL−1 in our experimental study.

Furthermore, we found that the variation of pH could effectively quench the fluorescence intensity of the NCDs solution in the absence and presence of cysteamine (Fig. S2B, in the absence of cysteamine, the fluorescence intensity of NCDs comparatively remains unchanged in a pH range from 5.0 to 7.0, whereas the fluorescence intensity of the NCDs had a tendency to increase with increasing the pH. The reason may be attributed to the presence of the carboxyl groups on the surface of the CDs that could improve conjugation in between surface functional groups of NCDs and consequently enhances the fluorescence intensity by introducing more surface defects. Importantly, the position of emission peaks remains unchanged with the variation of pH at a fixed concentration of NCDs.

The effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of the NCDs solution in the presence of cysteamine was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 5B, the fluorescence intensity of NCDs in the presence of cysteamine showed a similar trend as shown in the case of fluorescence intensity of NCDs. Although the maximum decrease in fluorescence intensity i.e., F0-F (F0= Fluorescence intensity of NCDs solution and F = fluorescence intensity of NCDs in the presence of cysteamine) was observed at pH 7.0. Therefore the pH 7.0 was chosen as an optimum pH of the solution for studying the sensing experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of interfering metal ions and amino acids
ce:section.ce:para:
In comparison to the other methods, we have developed a better sensing approach for cysteamine by inexpensive and quickly synthesized CDs based on less complicated fluorescence spectroscopy. Table 2 displays the comparison of several methods for detection of cysteamine. Various metal ions (such as Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Fe2+, Cu2+ and Fe3+) and amino acids (such as glycine,alanine, leucine, tryptophan, glutamine, serine, cysteine, glutamic acid, histidine) have essential roles in the human organism. Thus, the effect of these interfering metal ions in the presence of photoluminescent NCDs was inspected by fluorescence response in phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.0). As shown in Fig. 4
A, no apparent decrease of fluorescence intensity is observed for metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Fe2+ and Fe3+) except Cu2+. The reason for the interference of Cu2+ might be attributed to the complexing tendency of Cu2+ ions with amine groups those are present in NCDs surface in less amount [17].On the other hand, except glutathione and cysteine, other amino acids are unable to quench the fluorescence intensity of NCDs as shown in Fig. 4B. This is because cysteine (IEP 5.07) possesses one extra carboxylic acid group than cysteamine (pI 9.5) which decreases its IEP value.In case of glutathione, the IEP value is found to be around 5.97 facilitating the quenching in some extent which is slightly larger than cysteine but in comparison with Cysteamine, this seems to be very lower. Thus, the electrostatic interaction between cysteamine and NCDs (IEP 2.9) facilitates greater quenching of fluorescence intensity than that of cysteine. Even if, the interfering effect of Cu2+ ion and cysteine were similar to the effect of cysteamine when the concentration of both were 10-fold higher than that of cysteamine which makes NCDs highly selective to detect cysteamine in real samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake and cytotoxicity studies of NCDs
ce:section.ce:para: In the current study, cellular uptake efficiency of NCDs by MDA-MB-231 cells at different time periods is visualized using confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) as shown in Fig. 7. After 1 h of NCDs treatment, the cells display feeble blue, green and red fluorescence simultaneously, beneath cell membrane indicating the cellular uptake of the carrier (Fig. 5). In specific, after 3 h of laser exposure more scattered intensive blue fluorescence is observed throughout the cell. Interestingly, fluorescence intensity is found to be increased in the cytoplasm and nuclear periphery after 6 h of laser exposure. In addition to blue fluorescence, green and red fluorescence is also observed in cells subjected to NCDs treatments. This result is well inaccordance with fluorescence spectrometric analysis. Furthermore, the fluorescence is amplified on extended incubations (data not shown) substantiating NCDs accumulation inside the cell. Altogether, this study suggests cellular uptake behaviour of the NCDs proficiently.

The biocompatibility of NCDs is also assessed through cytotoxicity assay on MDA-MB-231 cell line (Fig. 6 ). Cells are treated over a range of NCDs concentrations (0–250 μg). The cell viability is decreased to 83% approximately in the sample on the nanoparticles treatment even at high concentration i.e., 250 μg/mL. These results reveal that the synthesized biocompatible hydrophilic NCDs can be selected as a promising photoluminescent sensors for the selective and sensitive detection of cysteamine in vivo and in vitro.

In summary, a simple and quick one-pot pyrolysis method was developed to fabricate water soluble photoluminescent N-doped carbon dots using tartaric acid and urea as precursors. The NCDs offered multicolour (blue, green and red) fluorescence property at various excitation wavelengths and it showed high stability at different pH and ionic environments. The fluorescence intensity of NCDs could be improved by increasing the concentration of passivating agent urea. The cellular uptake study and cytotoxicity assay of NCDs showed its high biocompatibility and minimum cell toxicity and thus it can be used as a fluorescent probe for the quantitative estimation of cysteamine with a wide range of 10–210 μM and detection limit of 75.6 nM in optimum conditions. The Stern–Volmer quenching constant (KSV) values were decreased with increasing the temperature, indicating that the fluorescence quenching of NCDs was driven by a static quenching mechanism. The zeta potential measurement showed that electrostatic interaction in between NCDs and cysteamine facilitates the formation of non-fluorescent ground state complex. This work clearly demonstrated that the synthesized photoluminescent hydrophilic NCDs provides anew platform for sensing cysteamine and may be applied in the clinical analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the DST-FIST funded XPS facility at the Department of Physics and Meteorology, IIT Kharagpur, India and Central Research Facility (CRF), IIT Kharagpur for providing infrastructural support for the characterization of the samples. S. Konar is thankful to the Indian Institute of Technology, Kharagpur for the fellowship received for carrying out the present research work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2019.01.117.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Suraj Konar received his B.Sc. degree from St. Xavier’s College Kolkata, M.Sc. degree from Presidency University Kolkata and received his PhD degree from department of chemistry, Indian Institute of technology, Kharagpur, India. He recently joined as an assistant professor in Department of Chemistry, R.D. & D.J. College Munger. He is majoring in preparation of nanomaterials and its application in catalysis, biosensing, drug delivery and protein fibril inhibitors.

B. N. Prashanth Kumar received his PhD degree from School of medical science and technology, Indian Institute of technology, Kharagpur, India. He presently is a Post-doctoral fellow in The University of Tennessee Health Science Center, Memphis, TN, United States. He is mainly engaged in the field of cancer biology.

Madhusudan Kr. Mahto received his B.Sc. degree and M.Sc. degree from Ranchi University, Jharkhand. He presently is a PhD student in department of chemistry, Indian Institute of technology, Kharagpur, India.

Dipanjan Samanta received his B.Sc. degree from University of Calcutta, India and M.Sc. degree from department of chemistry, Indian Institute of technology, Roorkee, India. He presently is a PhD student in department of chemistry, Indian Institute of technology, Kharagpur, India.

Md Abdus Salam Shaik received his B.Sc. degree from University of Calcutta, India and M.Sc. degree from department of chemistry, Jamia Millia Islamia, Delhi, India. He presently is a PhD student in department of chemistry, Indian Institute of technology, Kharagpur, India.

Manisha Shaw received his B.Sc. degree from University of Calcutta, India and M.Sc. degree from department of chemistry, Indian Institute of technology, Kharagpur, India. She presently is a PhD student in department of chemistry, Indian Institute of technology, Kharagpur, India.

Mahitosh Mandal received his PhD degree from Jadavpur University, Kolkata, India andhe presently is a Professor in School of medical science and technology, Indian Institute of technology, Kharagpur, India.He is mainly engaged in the field of cancer biology, drug delivery, Angiogenesis and signal transduction.

Amita Pathak is an associate professor in department of chemistry, Indian Institute of technology, Kharagpur, India. Her research interest comprises the development of new nanomaterialsand their applicationin catalysis, drug delivery, tissue engineering and biosensing.
